summary: Apple issued its first earnings warning since 2002, revising Q1 FY2019 revenue down to $84 billion from a projected $89-93 billion range — a massive $5-7 billion shortfall driven primarily by weakness in Greater China. Gruber argues that iPhone unit sales peaked years ago as Apple exhausted new markets to enter, and the maturing smartphone market means longer upgrade cycles. He criticizes Apple's decision to cite its battery replacement program as a factor in slower sales, calling it a poor framing that implies the old throttling behavior was designed to drive upgrades. He also notes Apple's terrible timing in choosing this quarter to stop reporting iPhone unit sales, which fuels suspicion they had something to hide. Gruber admits he was wrong to dismiss earlier reports of weak iPhone sales as supplier chain bullshit, though he maintains most of those reports were still poorly sourced.
key_points: Apple's $5-7 billion revenue miss is almost entirely attributable to Greater China and represents the company's first earnings warning in over 16 years
iPhone unit sales peaked years ago because Apple has exhausted new markets and the smartphone market is maturing, leading to longer upgrade cycles — similar to how MacBooks last 4-5 years
Apple citing its battery replacement program as a drag on sales is a self-own that implies the old throttling behavior conveniently drove upgrades
Stopping iPhone unit sale reporting in the same quarter sales cratered was catastrophically bad timing that even Gruber finds suspicious
The lack-of-innovation narrative is wrong — the real issue is market saturation and maturation, not product quality
money_quotes: This is bad news, pure and simple, and for Apple, truly extraordinary.
It's easy to say 'OK, it's just China', except that China is Apple's only hope for iPhone sales growth.

Apple should be proud that you can get two or three extra years of great performance from an older iPhone with a relatively inexpensive battery replacement, not pointing fingers at the program as a reason for slower sales this quarter.
The problem iPhone sales face is not a lack of innovation.
They're much in the position Microsoft got to with Windows and PCs — they're no longer an upstart growth company and are now a massively profitable blue chip.

Durable hardware with future-proof chip performance, combined with Apple's redoubled effort to make sure iOS 12 runs well on older hardware — these are good things for users and for the long-term reputation of the iPhone, but by definition they lengthen upgrade cycles.
Apple could not have picked a worse quarter in which to stop reporting iPhone unit sale numbers.
key_insight: Apple's iPhone revenue miss isn't a failure of innovation but the inevitable consequence of market saturation and maturation — the iPhone has simply reached everyone it can reach, and those who have one are keeping it longer.
